figures: Figure 1.1
High-level architecture of Spike-Sense, showing the data, model, service and presentation layers and the flow of telemetry from raw CSV series to dashboard, database and alerts.
Figure 2.1

A real NAB series, cpu_utilization_asg_misconfiguration, with its officially labelled anomaly window shown in red. The labelled fault falls in the final portion of the timeline, which under a chronological split becomes the held-out test data.
Figure 3.1
Use-case diagram showing the operations analyst and evaluator actors, the external Discord service, and the use cases provided within the Spike-Sense system boundary.
Figure 3.2
Level-0 (context) data-flow diagram. The system is a single process exchanging data with the NAB dataset, the analyst, and the Discord service.
Figure 3.3
Level-1 data-flow diagram decomposing the system into its six processes and four data stores.
Figure 4.1
Class diagram of the principal classes and structured records, including the model registry, the preprocessing and split records, the alert-state tracker, and the two persisted entities.
Figure 4.2
Entity–relationship diagram of the persistence layer. The PREDICTIONS entity is related one-to-many to the ALERTS entity through the prediction_id foreign key.
Figure 4.3
Sequence diagram of single-window scoring, from the dashboard request through scaling, both models, combination, persistence and alerting, to the response.
Figure 4.4
Sequence diagram of the spike-injection demonstration, showing injection, preprocessing, batch scoring and persistence of the triggering detection.
Figure 4.5
Activity diagram of the training pipeline, from per-series preprocessing through aggregation to the training and persistence of both models.
Figure 5.1
Automatically generated Swagger (OpenAPI) documentation of the Spike-Sense REST API, showing the System, History, Inference, Evaluation and Demo endpoint groups and the data schemas.
Figure 5.2
The Spike-Sense dashboard displaying a real EC2 CPU series. The top panel shows the metric with ground-truth anomalies; the lower panels show the two detectors’ signals.
Figure 5.3
The dashboard after injecting a point spike. The detectors flag the injected region; the lower panels show the corresponding rise in Isolation Forest score and LSTM reconstruction error.
Figure 5.4
JSON response of the /alerts endpoint, listing recent persisted alerts with their detecting models and delivery status.
Figure 7.1
LSTM Autoencoder training and validation loss per epoch. The best validation loss is reached at epoch six, where early stopping halts training.
Figure 7.2
Test-set precision, recall and F1-score for the four configurations. The union ensemble attains the highest F1 and recall.
Figure 7.3
Confusion matrices for the four configurations on the test set, showing the true/false positive and negative counts behind the headline metrics.
Figure 7.4
Precision–recall curves for the four configurations with their average-precision (AP) scores. The combined configurations achieve the highest AP.
Figure 7.5
Isolation Forest performance on validation data as the contamination parameter varies; lower values trade recall for substantially higher precision.
Figure 7.6
LSTM Autoencoder performance on validation data as the threshold percentile varies, showing the precision–recall trade-off the threshold controls.
Figure 7.7
Recall of each configuration on injected synthetic anomalies. The Isolation Forest detects point and level-shift anomalies with perfect recall and trend drift strongly.
Figure 7.8
The dashboard’s evaluation panel, showing the live test-set performance table together with the metric and detector charts.
tables: Comparison of the two detectors adopted in Spike-Sense.
Functional requirements of the Spike-Sense system.
Database schema: columns of the predictions and alerts tables.
REST API endpoints exposed by the FastAPI service layer.
Tools and technologies used in the implementation, with versions and roles.
Representative test cases and their outcomes (selected from 114 automated tests).
Test-set performance of the four detector configurations on 5,564 windows.
